skill.name: memory-orchestration
skill.description: Analyze context management, memory systems, and state continuity in agent frameworks. Use when (1) understanding how prompts are assembled, (2) evaluating eviction policies for context overflow, (3) mapping memory tiers (short-term/long-term), (4) analyzing token budget management, or (5) comparing context strategies across frameworks.
skill.summary: Analyze prompt assembly, memory tiers, eviction policies, and token budgets in agent frameworks.
skill.author: Dowwie

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported external command and weak cryptography patterns, but manual review found only Markdown examples, diagrams, and prose. The skill contains no executable scripts, network calls, environment access, prompt injection text, or instructions to run unsafe commands.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Misread as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Static external command detections occur inside Markdown code fences, inline package names, diagrams, and output templates. These locations document memory orchestration concepts and do not instruct the agent to execute shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visible Markdown documentation and illustrative Python or Markdown snippets. No backtick shell execution, subprocess call, or command string is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern in Plain Text
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections match ordinary words and template text, not cryptographic functions or hash algorithms. No MD5, SHA1, cipher setup, password hashing, or security-sensitive crypto API appears in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the flagged locations shows description text, comments, and output labels. There is no cryptographic operation to misuse.

content.value_statement: Agent frameworks often hide how prompts, memory, and token budgets interact. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured method for reviewing memory orchestration designs.
actual_capabilities: Maps prompt assembly order from system prompts through current user input.
Compares eviction strategies such as FIFO, sliding window, summarization, vector retrieval, and importance scoring.
Classifies memory tiers across in-prompt, session, and persistent storage layers.
Reviews token counting, budget allocation, and overflow handling approaches.
Produces a structured memory orchestration analysis for an agent framework.
limitations: Does not inspect a repository automatically without codebase context from the user or another skill.
Does not benchmark retrieval quality or token counts with live model APIs.
Does not implement memory storage, vector search, or prompt builders.
Does not validate privacy compliance for stored user data.

use_cases.title: Review an Agent Framework
use_cases.description: Identify how an existing framework assembles prompts, stores memory, and handles context overflow.

use_cases.title: Compare Memory Designs
use_cases.description: Evaluate tradeoffs between short-term context windows, summarization, and vector-backed persistent memory.

use_cases.title: Document Context Behavior
use_cases.description: Create a clear report that explains prompt order, memory tiers, token budgets, and overflow behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Identify Memory Components
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a single framework.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this agent framework for memory orchestration. List the prompt components, memory stores, and token budget rules you can identify.

prompt_templates.title: Trace Prompt Assembly
prompt_templates.prompt: Trace how this system builds the final model input. Show the order of system instructions, retrieved context, tools, history, user input, and scratchpad content.

prompt_templates.title: Evaluate Overflow Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this framework's context overflow behavior. Compare its eviction policy against sliding window, summarization, vector retrieval, and importance scoring.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Framework Strategies
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare memory orchestration across these agent frameworks. Build a decision matrix covering prompt assembly, memory tiers, token budgeting, persistence, and failure modes.

output_examples.input: Review a framework that keeps recent turns in memory and archives older turns to a vector store.
output_examples.output: The analysis identifies a sliding window for recent history, vector-backed persistent memory for older turns, and a retrieval step before prompt assembly. It notes that retrieval quality and privacy controls need separate review.

output_examples.input: Compare two agents where one truncates old messages and the other summarizes them.
output_examples.output: The comparison explains that truncation is simple and predictable but loses early context. Summarization preserves intent better but adds cost, latency, and possible summary drift.

output_examples.input: Document token management for an agent with tools, history, and output reserve.
output_examples.output: The report separates fixed prompt costs from history allocation and output reserve. It highlights where overflow triggers truncation, summarization, or an error.

best_practices: Review actual prompt construction code before drawing conclusions about memory behavior.
Separate working memory, session memory, and persistent memory in the final analysis.
Record both the overflow trigger and the action taken when token limits are reached.
anti_patterns: Assuming all stored context is retrieved without checking relevance filters.
Treating summarization as lossless when it can omit important details.
Ignoring privacy and retention implications for persistent user memory.

faq.question: What does this skill analyze?
faq.answer: It analyzes how agent frameworks assemble prompts, manage memory tiers, and handle token limits.

faq.question: Does it run code or commands?
faq.answer: No. The skill is a documentation guide and contains no executable automation.

faq.question: Can it compare multiple frameworks?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides categories for comparing prompt assembly, memory tiers, eviction policies, and token budgeting.

faq.question: Does it measure exact token usage?
faq.answer: No. It explains token management strategies but does not call tokenizers or model APIs.

faq.question: What input works best?
faq.answer: Provide code paths, architecture notes, or excerpts that show prompt building, memory storage, and context trimming.

faq.question: Is this a security auditing skill?
faq.answer: No. It can surface memory retention concerns, but it is mainly for architecture and context management analysis.
